execution_note: Results from cross_registry_check.js analysis of npm, PyPI, and Smithery registries for MCP server package name collisions.

pypi_packages_checked.name: mcp-server-filesystem
pypi_packages_checked.note: PyPI uses different naming convention (hyphen-separated, no scope). If this exists, it is NOT from Anthropic's official @modelcontextprotocol npm scope.

known_collision_risks.reason: Different registries use different naming conventions. npm uses scoped packages (@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem) while PyPI uses flat names (mcp-server-filesystem). An attacker can register the PyPI name to distribute malicious code to Python users expecting the official MCP filesystem server.
GitHub server handles OAuth tokens. A malicious PyPI package with the same name could steal GitHub credentials.
Fetch server has network access. Cross-registry confusion could lead to SSRF attacks.

attack_scenarios.description: Developer reads MCP docs referencing 'server-filesystem'. They pip install mcp-server-filesystem instead of npx @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem. If an attacker has registered the PyPI name, malicious code runs.
Attacker registers npm packages like 'modelcontextprotocol-server-filesystem' (without the @scope) or 'mcp-server-filesytem' (typo). Users who make typos get malicious packages.
Smithery registry does not use npm scoping. An attacker can register a server with the same name as an official npm package, potentially with different (malicious) code.

recommendations: MCP ecosystem should establish a cross-registry naming authority
Package managers should warn when installing MCP packages from unofficial sources
MCP-Lattice should verify package provenance across registries
Official packages should include signed attestations
Users should pin server versions and verify checksums
